answer:
Build a URL Shortener with Analytics Dashboard — 45-Minute Plan

What You Build and Why

A URL shortener with analytics is the ideal 45-minute project because it covers full-stack CRUD, redirect handling (301/302), click tracking (IP, user-agent, referrer, timestamp), and a real-time analytics dashboard. The interviewer evaluates: database schema design (short code uniqueness, click events), API design (create, redirect, stats), and frontend data visualization. The first 45 minutes focus on a working MVP: shorten URL → redirect with tracking → display click count and basic chart.

Manoj's Implementation Strategy

Drawing from ContactsHub's CRUD architecture (React + Fastify + MongoDB) and the CRM Dashboard's chart rendering with Recharts, Manoj would scaffold the URL shortener using his proven stack. The short code generation uses the same crypto.randomBytes pattern he used for JWT token generation in the HR Leave System. The analytics dashboard mirrors the CRM's sales pipeline charts — Recharts line chart for clicks over time, bar chart for top referrers. The redirect endpoint is similar to the HR Leave System's notification deep-link handler.

45-Minute Build Plan

Implementation: Core Backend + Frontend

// Backend: Fastify URL Shortener
import Fastify from 'fastify';
import crypto from 'crypto';
import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';

const app = Fastify({ logger: true });
const prisma = new PrismaClient();

// Create short URL
app.post('/api/shorten', async (req, reply) => {
  const { url } = req.body;
  
  // Validate URL
  try { new URL(url); } catch {
    return reply.status(400).send({ error: 'Invalid URL' });
  }
  
  // Generate unique short code (8 hex chars = 4 billion combinations)
  let shortCode;
  let attempts = 0;
  do {
    shortCode = crypto.randomBytes(4).toString('hex');
    const existing = await prisma.url.findUnique({ where: { shortCode } });
    if (!existing) break;
    attempts++;
  } while (attempts < 3);
  
  const shortened = await prisma.url.create({
    data: { shortCode, originalUrl: url }
  });
  
  return reply.status(201).send({
    shortUrl: `${process.env.BASE_URL}/${shortCode}`,
    shortCode,
    originalUrl: url
  });
});

// Redirect + track click
app.get('/:code', async (req, reply) => {
  const url = await prisma.url.findUnique({
    where: { shortCode: req.params.code }
  });
  
  if (!url) return reply.status(404).send({ error: 'URL not found' });
  
  // Track click asynchronously — don't block redirect
  prisma.click.create({
    data: {
      urlId: url.id,
      ip: req.ip,
      userAgent: req.headers['user-agent'] || '',
      referrer: req.headers['referer'] || '',
    }
  }).catch(err => app.log.error('Click tracking failed:', err));
  
  // 302 redirect (not 301 — prevents browser caching)
  return reply.redirect(302, url.originalUrl);
});

// Analytics endpoint
app.get('/api/urls/:code/stats', async (req, reply) => {
  const url = await prisma.url.findUnique({
    where: { shortCode: req.params.code }
  });
  if (!url) return reply.status(404).send({ error: 'Not found' });
  
  const [totalClicks, uniqueIPs, clicksByDay] = await Promise.all([
    prisma.click.count({ where: { urlId: url.id } }),
    prisma.click.groupBy({
      by: ['ip'], where: { urlId: url.id }, _count: true
    }).then(r => r.length),
    prisma.$queryRaw`
      SELECT DATE(created_at) as date, COUNT(*)::int as clicks
      FROM clicks WHERE url_id = ${url.id}
      GROUP BY DATE(created_at) ORDER BY date DESC LIMIT 30`
  ]);
  
  return reply.send({
    shortCode: url.shortCode, originalUrl: url.originalUrl,
    totalClicks, uniqueIPs,
    clicksByDay, createdAt: url.createdAt
  });
});

// Frontend: React Dashboard
function URLShortener() {
  const [url, setUrl] = useState('');
  const [result, setResult] = useState(null);
  const [stats, setStats] = useState(null);
  
  const shorten = async () => {
    const res = await fetch('/api/shorten', {
      method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
      body: JSON.stringify({ url })
    });
    const data = await res.json();
    setResult(data);
    // Load stats immediately
    const statsRes = await fetch(`/api/urls/${data.shortCode}/stats`);
    setStats(await statsRes.json());
  };
  
  return (
    

      

         setUrl(e.target.value)}
          placeholder="Paste long URL" className="flex-1 border p-2 rounded" />
        
      

      
      {result && (
        

          
Short URL: {result.shortUrl}

          
        

      )}
      
      {stats && (
        

          

            

              
{stats.totalClicks}

              
Total Clicks

            

            

              
{stats.uniqueIPs}

              
Unique Visitors

            

          

          
            
            
            
          
        

      )}
    

  );
}

Manoj's Implementation Insights

From building the CRM Dashboard, Manoj knows that the analytics chart must handle sparse data gracefully — days with zero clicks should show as zero, not be missing from the chart. He'd use a date-fill utility to ensure continuous x-axis data. From the HR Leave System's notification tracking, he knows that async fire-and-forget database writes can silently fail — the .catch() handler logs errors without blocking the redirect. For the short code generation, he'd explain the collision probability: with 8 hex chars (4 billion combinations) and expected < 1M URLs, collision probability is < 0.02% — but the retry loop handles it safely.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
301 redirect instead of 302Browsers cache redirect — clicks not countedUse 302 (temporary redirect) for accurate tracking
Synchronous click trackingRedirect delayed by DB insert latencyFire-and-forget click insert — don't await
Short code collision uncheckedURL overwrite — users redirected to wrong siteUnique constraint + retry loop (max 3 attempts)
Raw click scan for analyticsSlow at 10M+ clicks — full table scanPre-aggregate hourly/daily in materialized view
No URL validationUsers shorten invalid/malicious URLsValidate with new URL(); optional: check against phishing DB

Validation Strategy

  • End-to-end test: Create short URL → access it → verify redirect AND click recorded
  • Analytics accuracy: Generate 100 clicks → verify stats show exactly 100
  • Collision handling: Mock randomBytes to return same value → verify retry succeeds
  • Invalid URL rejection: Submit "not-a-url" → verify 400 error
  • Performance: Redirect endpoint < 20ms (click tracking is async)

Build Interview Scheduling Service with Conflict Detection — 45-Minute Plan

What You Build and Why

An interview scheduling service tests temporal data modeling (time slots, timezones), conflict detection algorithms (overlapping intervals), multi-party coordination (interviewer + candidate availability), and state machine (scheduled → confirmed → completed → feedback). The 45-minute MVP: create interview slots, book a candidate into a slot, detect time conflicts, and display a calendar view.

Manoj's Implementation Strategy

From the HR Leave Management System, Manoj has direct experience with temporal conflict detection — the leave system checks for overlapping leave dates (no weekends, no backdating, overlap detection) using the same interval-overlap algorithm needed here. The 3-level approval workflow maps directly to interview scheduling states. The CRM Dashboard's pipeline view (Kanban-style cards) informs the calendar UI — each interview slot is a draggable card. Socket.io from the HR system enables real-time slot availability updates when another user books a slot.

45-Minute Build Plan

Implementation: Core Scheduling + Conflict Detection

// Fastify interview scheduling service

// Conflict detection — core algorithm
async function checkConflicts(interviewerId, candidateId, startTime, endTime, excludeId) {
  const conflicts = await prisma.interview.findMany({
    where: {
      id: excludeId ? { not: excludeId } : undefined,
      status: { in: ['scheduled', 'confirmed'] },
      startTime: { lt: endTime },  // Overlap condition
      endTime: { gt: startTime },
      OR: [
        { interviewerId },  // Interviewer double-booked
        { candidateId }     // Candidate double-booked
      ]
    },
    include: {
      interviewer: { select: { name: true } },
      candidate: { select: { name: true } }
    }
  });
  
  return conflicts;
}

// Create interview with conflict check (atomic)
app.post('/api/interviews', async (req, reply) => {
  const { interviewerId, candidateId, startTime, endTime, type } = req.body;
  
  // Validate: no past dates, duration 30-120 min, within business hours
  const start = new Date(startTime);
  const end = new Date(endTime);
  const durationMin = (end - start) / 60000;
  
  if (start < new Date()) return reply.status(400).send({ error: 'Cannot schedule in the past' });
  if (durationMin < 30 || durationMin > 120) return reply.status(400).send({ error: 'Duration must be 30-120 minutes' });
  
  // Transaction: check conflicts + create (prevents race condition)
  const result = await prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
    const conflicts = await tx.interview.findMany({
      where: {
        status: { in: ['scheduled', 'confirmed'] },
        startTime: { lt: end },
        endTime: { gt: start },
        OR: [{ interviewerId }, { candidateId }]
      }
    });
    
    if (conflicts.length > 0) {
      throw new Error(JSON.stringify({
        type: 'CONFLICT',
        conflicts: conflicts.map(c => ({
          id: c.id, startTime: c.startTime, endTime: c.endTime,
          reason: c.interviewerId === interviewerId ? 'Interviewer busy' : 'Candidate busy'
        }))
      }));
    }
    
    return tx.interview.create({
      data: { interviewerId, candidateId, startTime: start, endTime: end, type, status: 'scheduled' },
      include: { interviewer: { select: { name: true } }, candidate: { select: { name: true } } }
    });
  });
  
  return reply.status(201).send(result);
});

// Get availability for an interviewer on a given date
app.get('/api/availability', async (req, reply) => {
  const { interviewerId, date } = req.query;
  const dayStart = new Date(`${date}T09:00:00`);
  const dayEnd = new Date(`${date}T18:00:00`);
  
  const booked = await prisma.interview.findMany({
    where: {
      interviewerId,
      status: { in: ['scheduled', 'confirmed'] },
      startTime: { gte: dayStart },
      endTime: { lte: dayEnd }
    },
    orderBy: { startTime: 'asc' }
  });
  
  // Generate 30-min slots and mark availability
  const slots = [];
  for (let t = dayStart.getTime(); t < dayEnd.getTime(); t += 30 * 60000) {
    const slotStart = new Date(t);
    const slotEnd = new Date(t + 30 * 60000);
    const isBooked = booked.some(b => b.startTime < slotEnd && b.endTime > slotStart);
    slots.push({ start: slotStart, end: slotEnd, available: !isBooked });
  }
  
  return reply.send({ interviewerId, date, slots });
});

// React: Simple calendar day view
function InterviewCalendar({ interviewerId, date }) {
  const { data: availability } = useQuery(
    ['availability', interviewerId, date],
    () => fetch(`/api/availability?interviewerId=${interviewerId}&date=${date}`)
      .then(r => r.json())
  );
  
  return (
    

      {availability?.slots.map(slot => (
        
 slot.available && bookSlot(slot)}>
          {new Date(slot.start).toLocaleTimeString([], { hour: '2-digit', minute: '2-digit' })}
          {slot.available ? ' — Available' : ' — Booked'}
        

      ))}
    

  );
}

Manoj's Implementation Insights

From the HR Leave System, Manoj learned that overlap detection must use strict inequalities for start and non-strict for end, or vice versa — to avoid false conflicts on adjacent time slots. If slot A ends at 10:30 and slot B starts at 10:30, they're adjacent, not overlapping. The condition A.start < B.end AND A.end > B.start correctly handles this. He also learned from the leave system's concurrent approval bug: without a database transaction, two users could book the same slot simultaneously (both check for conflicts, both find none, both insert). The $transaction with serializable isolation level prevents this race condition.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Race condition on concurrent bookingDouble-booked interviewerDatabase transaction with row-level locking
Adjacent slots flagged as conflictsCan't book back-to-back interviewsStrict inequality: start < existingEnd AND end > existingStart
Timezone handlingInterview at wrong time for remote candidatesStore all times in UTC; convert to local timezone on display
No buffer between interviewsInterviewer has no breakAdd configurable buffer (15 min) — check conflict with expanded window
Past date scheduling allowedMeaningless data in systemValidate startTime > now at API level

Validation Strategy

  • Conflict detection: Book 10:00-10:30, attempt 10:15-10:45 → verify conflict returned
  • Adjacent slots: Book 10:00-10:30, attempt 10:30-11:00 → verify no conflict
  • Race condition: Two concurrent bookings for same slot → verify only one succeeds
  • Availability API: Book 3 slots → verify remaining slots show as available
  • Status transitions: scheduled → confirmed → completed (valid) vs completed → scheduled (invalid)

Implement Collaborative Document Comments with Realtime Updates — 45-Minute Plan

What You Build and Why

Collaborative document comments test real-time synchronization (WebSocket-based comment streaming), threaded discussions (nested replies), optimistic UI updates (show comment immediately, confirm server-side), and presence indicators (who's viewing the document). The 45-minute MVP: add comments to a document, reply to comments (threaded), see comments appear in real-time for all viewers, and show typing indicators.

Manoj's Implementation Strategy

Drawing directly from the HR Leave System's Socket.io implementation, Manoj would build real-time comment delivery using room-based namespacing — each document is a Socket.io room (doc:${documentId}). The threaded comment structure mirrors the leave approval chain (parent-child relationship). From ContactsHub's React architecture, he'd use React Query for server state management with optimistic updates — the comment appears instantly in the UI before the server confirms, exactly like the contact edit flow in ContactsHub.

45-Minute Build Plan

Implementation: Real-time Comments

// Backend: Comment CRUD + Socket.io
io.on('connection', (socket) => {
  // Join document room
  socket.on('doc:join', async (docId) => {
    socket.join(`doc:${docId}`);
    socket.docId = docId;
    
    // Announce presence
    io.to(`doc:${docId}`).emit('presence:update', {
      userId: socket.userId, name: socket.userName, action: 'joined'
    });
  });
  
  // Handle new comment
  socket.on('comment:create', async (data) => {
    const { docId, content, parentId } = data;
    
    const comment = await prisma.comment.create({
      data: {
        documentId: docId, content,
        authorId: socket.userId,
        parentId: parentId || null
      },
      include: { author: { select: { id: true, name: true, avatar: true } } }
    });
    
    // Broadcast to all viewers (including sender for confirmation)
    io.to(`doc:${docId}`).emit('comment:new', comment);
    
    // Acknowledge to sender with server-assigned ID
    socket.emit('comment:ack', { tempId: data.tempId, id: comment.id });
  });
  
  // Typing indicators
  socket.on('typing:start', ({ docId }) => {
    socket.to(`doc:${docId}`).emit('typing', {
      userId: socket.userId, name: socket.userName
    });
  });
  
  socket.on('disconnect', () => {
    if (socket.docId) {
      io.to(`doc:${socket.docId}`).emit('presence:update', {
        userId: socket.userId, action: 'left'
      });
    }
  });
});

// REST endpoint for initial load
app.get('/api/documents/:docId/comments', async (req, reply) => {
  const comments = await prisma.comment.findMany({
    where: { documentId: req.params.docId },
    include: { author: { select: { id: true, name: true, avatar: true } } },
    orderBy: { createdAt: 'asc' }
  });
  
  // Build thread tree
  const rootComments = comments.filter(c => !c.parentId);
  const replies = comments.filter(c => c.parentId);
  
  const threads = rootComments.map(root => ({
    ...root,
    replies: replies.filter(r => r.parentId === root.id)
  }));
  
  return reply.send(threads);
});

// Frontend: React comment component with optimistic updates
function CommentThread({ docId }) {
  const [comments, setComments] = useState([]);
  const [typingUsers, setTypingUsers] = useState([]);
  const socket = useSocket();
  
  useEffect(() => {
    // Load initial comments
    fetch(`/api/documents/${docId}/comments`)
      .then(r => r.json()).then(setComments);
    
    // Join document room
    socket.emit('doc:join', docId);
    
    // Listen for new comments
    socket.on('comment:new', (comment) => {
      setComments(prev => {
        if (comment.parentId) {
          return prev.map(thread =>
            thread.id === comment.parentId
              ? { ...thread, replies: [...(thread.replies || []), comment] }
              : thread
          );
        }
        return [...prev, { ...comment, replies: [] }];
      });
    });
    
    socket.on('typing', ({ userId, name }) => {
      setTypingUsers(prev => [...prev.filter(u => u.userId !== userId), { userId, name }]);
      setTimeout(() => setTypingUsers(prev => prev.filter(u => u.userId !== userId)), 3000);
    });
    
    return () => { socket.off('comment:new'); socket.off('typing'); };
  }, [docId]);
  
  const addComment = (content, parentId = null) => {
    const tempId = `temp-${Date.now()}`;
    
    // Optimistic update
    const optimistic = {
      id: tempId, content, parentId, pending: true,
      author: { name: 'You' }, createdAt: new Date()
    };
    
    if (parentId) {
      setComments(prev => prev.map(t =>
        t.id === parentId ? { ...t, replies: [...(t.replies || []), optimistic] } : t
      ));
    } else {
      setComments(prev => [...prev, { ...optimistic, replies: [] }]);
    }
    
    socket.emit('comment:create', { docId, content, parentId, tempId });
  };
  
  return (
    

      {comments.map(thread => (
         addComment(content, thread.id)} />
      ))}
      {typingUsers.length > 0 && (
        

          {typingUsers.map(u => u.name).join(', ')} typing...
        

      )}
       addComment(content)} />
    

  );
}

Manoj's Implementation Insights

From the HR Leave System's Socket.io implementation, Manoj knows that typing indicators must be debounced (300ms) and auto-expired (3s) — without auto-expiry, if a user stops typing without clearing the indicator (e.g., switches tabs), the "typing..." message persists indefinitely. He also learned from the HR system's notification delivery bug that Socket.io room membership must be tracked — when a user refreshes the page, they get a new socket but need to re-join the document room. The doc:join event in the connection handler ensures this. For the threaded structure, he'd build the tree server-side to avoid O(n²) client-side tree construction.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
No optimistic UIComment appears after 200ms delay — feels laggyShow comment immediately with pending indicator; confirm via ACK
Typing indicator never expires"Manoj typing..." shown forever after tab switchAuto-expire after 3s; clear on disconnect
Flat comment list (no threading)Can't follow conversation contextparentId-based threading; build tree server-side
No duplicate message preventionNetwork retry shows comment twicetempId → server ACK replaces optimistic with confirmed
Room not re-joined on reconnectUser stops receiving real-time updatesRe-emit doc:join on Socket.io reconnect event

Validation Strategy

  • Real-time delivery: Two browser tabs on same document → comment in Tab A appears in Tab B within 500ms
  • Threading: Reply to comment → verify it appears nested under parent
  • Optimistic UI: Slow network (DevTools throttle) → verify comment appears instantly with pending state
  • Typing indicator: Type in one tab → verify indicator appears in other tab → stop typing → verify indicator disappears after 3s
  • Presence: Open/close document → verify join/leave events broadcast

Create Notification Preference Center with Audit History — 45-Minute Plan

What You Build and Why

A notification preference center tests settings management patterns (per-channel toggles with granular controls), audit trail design (who changed what, when), real-time preference enforcement (preferences must be checked before every notification send), and UI state management (complex form with nested toggles). The 45-minute MVP: per-channel notification toggles (email, in-app, push), quiet hours configuration, preference change audit log, and immediate enforcement.

Manoj's Implementation Strategy

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj built a simpler version of notification preferences — users could opt out of email notifications for leave status updates while keeping in-app Socket.io notifications active. The audit history requirement maps directly to the leave system's approval audit trail (who approved/rejected, when, with what reason). From ContactsHub's settings page, he has experience with React Hook Form for complex nested form state — toggle groups, time pickers, and conditional fields. The preference center extends this pattern with per-notification-type granularity.

45-Minute Build Plan

Implementation: Preference CRUD with Audit Trail

// Backend: Preference management with audit
const DEFAULT_PREFERENCES = {
  'leave_status': { email: true, inApp: true, push: false },
  'approval_request': { email: true, inApp: true, push: true },
  'system_announcement': { email: true, inApp: true, push: false },
  'report_ready': { email: true, inApp: true, push: false }
};

// Get preferences (with defaults for new users)
app.get('/api/preferences', async (req, reply) => {
  let prefs = await prisma.notificationPreference.findUnique({
    where: { userId: req.user.sub }
  });
  
  if (!prefs) {
    prefs = await prisma.notificationPreference.create({
      data: {
        userId: req.user.sub,
        channels: DEFAULT_PREFERENCES,
        quietHours: { enabled: false, start: '22:00', end: '08:00' }
      }
    });
  }
  
  return reply.send(prefs);
});

// Update preferences with diff-based audit
app.put('/api/preferences', async (req, reply) => {
  const { channels, quietHours } = req.body;
  const userId = req.user.sub;
  
  // Get current preferences
  const current = await prisma.notificationPreference.findUnique({
    where: { userId }
  });
  
  // Generate audit entries by diffing old vs new
  const auditEntries = [];
  
  for (const [notifType, channelPrefs] of Object.entries(channels)) {
    for (const [channel, enabled] of Object.entries(channelPrefs)) {
      const oldValue = current?.channels?.[notifType]?.[channel];
      if (oldValue !== enabled) {
        auditEntries.push({
          userId,
          field: `${notifType}.${channel}`,
          oldValue: String(oldValue ?? 'default'),
          newValue: String(enabled),
          changedAt: new Date()
        });
      }
    }
  }
  
  // Check quiet hours changes
  if (JSON.stringify(current?.quietHours) !== JSON.stringify(quietHours)) {
    auditEntries.push({
      userId,
      field: 'quietHours',
      oldValue: JSON.stringify(current?.quietHours),
      newValue: JSON.stringify(quietHours),
      changedAt: new Date()
    });
  }
  
  // Transactional update: preferences + audit entries
  await prisma.$transaction([
    prisma.notificationPreference.upsert({
      where: { userId },
      create: { userId, channels, quietHours },
      update: { channels, quietHours }
    }),
    ...auditEntries.map(entry =>
      prisma.preferenceAudit.create({ data: entry })
    )
  ]);
  
  // Invalidate cached preferences
  await redis.del(`prefs:${userId}`);
  
  return reply.send({ updated: true, changes: auditEntries.length });
});

// Get audit history
app.get('/api/preferences/audit', async (req, reply) => {
  const audits = await prisma.preferenceAudit.findMany({
    where: { userId: req.user.sub },
    orderBy: { changedAt: 'desc' },
    take: 50
  });
  return reply.send(audits);
});

// Preference-aware notification send (used by NotificationService)
async function shouldSendNotification(userId, notifType, channel) {
  // Check Redis cache first
  let prefs = await redis.get(`prefs:${userId}`);
  if (prefs) {
    prefs = JSON.parse(prefs);
  } else {
    const dbPrefs = await prisma.notificationPreference.findUnique({ where: { userId } });
    prefs = dbPrefs?.channels ?? DEFAULT_PREFERENCES;
    await redis.setex(`prefs:${userId}`, 300, JSON.stringify(prefs));
  }
  
  return prefs[notifType]?.[channel] ?? true; // Default to enabled
}

// React: Preference center UI
function PreferenceCenter() {
  const { data: prefs, refetch } = useQuery(['preferences'], () =>
    fetch('/api/preferences').then(r => r.json())
  );
  const { data: audit } = useQuery(['audit'], () =>
    fetch('/api/preferences/audit').then(r => r.json())
  );
  
  const [channels, setChannels] = useState({});
  const [quietHours, setQuietHours] = useState({ enabled: false, start: '22:00', end: '08:00' });
  
  useEffect(() => {
    if (prefs) { setChannels(prefs.channels); setQuietHours(prefs.quietHours); }
  }, [prefs]);
  
  const save = async () => {
    await fetch('/api/preferences', {
      method: 'PUT', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
      body: JSON.stringify({ channels, quietHours })
    });
    refetch();
  };
  
  return (
    

      
Notification Preferences

      
          {Object.entries(channels).map(([type, ch]) => (
            
              {['email', 'inApp', 'push'].map(channel => (
                
              ))}
            
          ))}
        

        
TypeEmailIn-AppPush

        

              {type.replace('_', ' ')}
                   setChannels(prev => ({
                      ...prev, [type]: { ...prev[type], [channel]: e.target.checked }
                    }))}
                  />
                

      

      
      
      
Change History

      
    {audit?.map(a => (
        
  • {a.field}: {a.oldValue} → {a.newValue} ({new Date(a.changedAt).toLocaleString()})
    • 
      ))}

    

  );
}

Manoj's Implementation Insights

From the HR Leave System, Manoj learned that preference enforcement must happen at the notification service layer, not the UI layer. Initially, the HR system checked preferences on the frontend before triggering notifications — but this meant server-side automated notifications (like cron-triggered reminders) bypassed preference checks entirely. He moved the check to the backend's NotificationService.send() method, ensuring all notification channels respect preferences regardless of trigger source. The audit trail implementation reuses the same pattern as the leave approval audit — every state change is recorded with who, what, when, and old/new values.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Preferences checked on frontend onlyServer-side notifications bypass preferencesCheck in backend NotificationService — single enforcement point
No default preferences for new usersNew users get zero notifications or all notificationsDEFAULT_PREFERENCES applied on first access; sensible defaults
Stale cached preferencesUser changes preference but notifications don't changeInvalidate Redis cache on preference update; 5-min TTL as safety net
No audit trailCan't debug why user stopped receiving notificationsDiff-based audit on every preference change; immutable audit log
Quiet hours not timezone-awareUser's "10 PM" is different timezone than serverStore timezone with preferences; convert before enforcement

Validation Strategy

  • Preference enforcement: Disable email for leave_status → trigger leave notification → verify no email sent
  • Audit accuracy: Change 3 preferences → verify exactly 3 audit entries created
  • Default preferences: New user → verify sensible defaults applied
  • Cache invalidation: Update preference → verify next notification respects new setting (not cached old one)
  • Quiet hours: Set quiet hours 10PM-8AM → send notification at 11PM → verify deferred to 8AM
